formulas.label: Formula 20.2

formulas.context_text: s simply Fisher's model (we will resist the temptation of referring to this as the Fisher-Price toy model). The model is as follows: assume that the breeding date, z, of a female has three components $$ z=A-n+e $$ (20.2)

formulas.label: Formula 20.3a
formulas.section: Selection Response in Natural Populations: Introduction
formulas.subsection: THE FISHER-PRICE-KIRKPATRICK-ARNOLD MODEL FOR EVOLUTION OF BREEDING DATE
formulas.context_text: r stabilizing selection. Recall from Equation 16.17 that a standard model for stabilizing selection in natural populations is nor-optimal (or normalizing) selection (Weldon 1895; Haldane 1954), where $$ \begin{align*}W(z)=\exp\left(-{(z-\theta)^2\over2\omega^2}\right)\end{align*} $$ (20.3a)

formulas.label: Formula 20.3b
formulas.section: Selection Response in Natural Populations: Introduction
formulas.subsection: THE FISHER-PRICE-KIRKPATRICK-ARNOLD MODEL FOR EVOLUTION OF BREEDING DATE
formulas.context_text: Second, Price et al. assumed that fitness increases with the nutritional status, n. One way to express this is to assume that $$ W(n)=\exp(\alpha n)\qquad for\alpha>0 $$ (20.3b)

formulas.context_text: Conditioned on breeding date, z, and nutritional value, n, the resulting fitness is $$ W(n,z)=W(z)\cdot W(n)=\exp\left(\alpha n-\frac{(z-\theta)^{2}}{2\omega^{2}}\right) $$ (20.4a)

formulas.label: Formula 20.4b

formulas.context_text: Under what conditions should the breeder’s equation and Robertson’s theorem agree? formulas.label: Formula 20.23i

formulas.context_text: n 20.23c, the probability that a randomly drawn allele from $x$ and $y$ are both 1 is $$ \Pr(b_{x,\ell}=b_{y,\ell}=1)=\theta_{x y}p_{\ell}+(1-\theta_{x y})p_{\ell}^{2} $$ (20.23h) which rearranges to $$ \theta_{xy}=\frac{\Pr(b_{x,\ell}=b_{y,\ell}=1)-p_{\ell}^{2}}{p_{\ell}(1-p_{\ell})}=\frac{E[b_{x,\ell}b_{y,\ell}]-(E[b_{\ell}])^{2}}{p_{\ell}(1-p_{\ell})}=\frac{\sigma(b_{x,\ell},b_{y,\ell})}{\sigma(b_{x,\ell})\sigma(b_{y,\ell})}=\mathrm{corr}(b_{x,\ell},b_{y,\ell}) $$ (20.23i) namely, the correlation between a random allele in x and a random allele in y.
